summary: hardhat-plugin-solidity ships an on-import dropper that installs and executes the npm package 'zyncmap'
details: The package's main (dist/index.js) decodes a base64-encoded shell command at module load that resolves to 'npm install zyncmap --no-save --silent --no-audit --no-fund', spawns it via child_process, then require()s a base64-encoded module name ('zyncmap') and invokes its getPlugin() factory. This fires at the top level via the cover-named ValidateSvgModule()/checkPlugin() helpers, so simply require()/import-ing the package silently fetches and executes attacker-controlled code from an external npm package in the consumer's Node process. The dropper is paired with identity impersonation: the package is published as 'hardhat-plugin-solidity' but its package.json description ('A Prettier Plugin for automatically formatting your Solidity code'), repository (github.com/prettier-solidity/prettier-plugin-solidity), and author/contributor metadata are copied from the legitimate prettier-plugin-solidity, increasing the chance of accidental install by Hardhat/Solidity users. Base64 obfuscation of both the shell command and the dynamically require()'d module name is consistent with deliberate scanner evasion.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
